summary: Dan Shapiro discusses Milwaukee Tools' announcement of a new 28-volt lithium-ion battery powered cordless tool line. He explains the technical reason the industry hadn't adopted li-ion batteries sooner: while they're lighter and pack more capacity, nickel-cadmium batteries deliver more power instantaneously, which matters for high-draw tools like saws. He expresses cautious optimism about the new tools while lamenting that Milwaukee has been acquired by TTI, a Hong Kong company that also owns Ryobi, marking another American toolmaker moving overseas.
key_points: Milwaukee Tools introduced a 28V lithium-ion cordless tool line, a significant shift from nickel-cadmium batteries
Li-ion batteries are lighter with more capacity, but ni-cad batteries historically delivered superior instantaneous power for high-current applications like saws
The key open question is whether Milwaukee solved the high current draw limitation of li-ion technology
Milwaukee's acquisition by TTI (a Hong Kong company that owns Ryobi) represents the continuing trend of US toolmakers moving overseas
money_quotes: li-ion batteries have more power, but ni-cads have always been able to deliver more power *at once*.
If you're running a saw, it's all about dumping as much power into the motor as you can.
Here's hoping they got it right, because I'd love to shed a few pounds off my cordless drill.
It's sad to see yet another US toolmaker heading overseas.
key_insight: The shift from ni-cad to lithium-ion in power tools wasn't just a matter of adopting better battery chemistry—it required solving the fundamental engineering challenge of instantaneous power delivery for high-current applications.
